rows.item: 学習意欲・授業参加度

summary:
This course traces the evolution of the English language over its 1500 year history. It begin by looking at its Proto Indo European roots, which are common to all European languages, and move chronologically through four main phases—Old English, Middle English, Early Modern English, and Modern English. We will look at how and why the language has changed over that time, and how it is still evolving today.
Each week we will begin with a lecture focusing on a different stage in its evolution. We will look at the literature from these periods, patterns of language use and so on.
We will also do in-class activities so that you are actively involved! As always, I invite questions from you at any time, or points of interest. I will also ask you questions as I am a firm believer in active learning!
aims: Participants will learn about how Engish has developed over the past 1500 years by examining its origins and main developmental phases, as well as the major linguistic, economic, social and personal influences on the language.
goals: Students will develop a clear understanding of strategies that can be used to facilitate communication in global contexts
Students will develop a clear understanding of strategies that can be used to facilitate communication in global contexts
Students will develop a clear understanding of strategies that can be used to facilitate communication in global contexts

plan.text: Roots: The Indo-European proto-languages; Germanic languages; Anglo-Frisian

Beginnings: Olde Englisch; the Celtic Languages; the Viking threat and Alfred the Great—the ‘saviour’ of English
Key texts: Beowulf, the Lord’s Prayer

Beginnings: The Norman invasion (1066) and French influence; Anglo- Norman
Key texts: The Domesday Book

Beginnings: Geoffrey Chaucer - the father of English Literature; the Great Vowel Shift Classroom activities
Key text: The Canterbury Tales, Piers Plowman
Classroom activities

The Elizabethan Era; Shakespeare—his work and legacy
Classroom activities
Key texts: Hamlet, Dr. Faustus

Lecture: The Renaissance and its influence on English
Classroom activities
English goes global: Exploration, colonialism and its role in the spread of English; dialects of the Anglosphere; loan words

Early Modern English (1700 ~); the King James Bible; early grammar texts (Lowth, Murray and Priestley); Samuel Johnson and the first English dictionary
Classroom activities

Late Modern English; the influence of the Industrial Revolution
Classroom activities

Present-Day English; English in the 20th Century; from English to Englishes; circles of English
Classroom activities

Lexis: the rapid expansion of the English lexicon
Classroom activities

Convergence and the influence of American English
Classroom activities
‘I was like, ‘Oh my God!’’; recent developments in speech; ‘text-speak’ and the influence of technology; ’couching’
Language barriers; restrictions on language; political correctness, and taboo words; the Business of English; commodification of the language

What is the future of English?
Classroom activities

textbooks: Materials for this class will be distributed by the instructor
prereq: This course is aimed at those with an interest in learning about the Indo-European origins of the English language, evolution through contact with other languages, and its current position as the global lingua franca. It should appeal to students of linguistics, English language education, and also visiting students. The course is taught in English and participants will need to be able to actively comprehend and communicate in English.
prep: Participants will be assessed on a formative basis through weekly online assignments, to be completed by the deadlines
teachers: ◎ マイケル シャープ

extra.label: 成績評価に関する補足
extra.text:
This course uses a formative system of evaluation. Participants are expected to complete weekly quizzes based on the content of the weekly lectures (each quiz is ~5%), as well as in-class activities.
このコースでは形成評価システムを使用します。受講者は毎週の講義内容に基づく毎週の小テスト（各小テストは5%程度）と、授業中の活動に出席することが求められます。
